articles.title: No panic, just precaution: Centre downplays May heat fears, advises calm and preparedness - Odisha TV
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Odisha
articles.full_text:
Amid rising concerns over soaring temperatures, the Centre on Saturday urged citizens not to panic over heatwave forecasts, asserting that the situation remains manageable with awareness and basic precautions. Union Minister Jitendra Singh said that while certain regions may experience above-normal temperatures in May, the conditions are neither uniform across the country nor beyond control.
Addressing the media, the Minister clarified that the government’s advisory is based on scientific inputs from the India Meteorological Department (IMD), and stressed that misinterpretation of forecasts often leads to unnecessary alarm. He emphasised that India has significantly improved its weather prediction capabilities, enabling timely alerts and better preparedness.
Heatwave Pattern Not Uniform
According to IMD’s monthly outlook, above-normal maximum temperatures are expected over parts of southern peninsular India, the northeast, and northwest India. However, large parts of the country may witness normal to below-normal temperatures, particularly during phases influenced by rainfall and thunderstorms.
Heatwave conditions are likely to affect Himalayan foothills, parts of the east coast including Odisha, Coastal Andhra Pradesh and Tamil Nadu, as well as Gujarat and Maharashtra. These regions may see 2–4 additional heatwave days above normal during the month.
Fluctuating Temperature Phases
The forecast indicates two peak heat phases:
Second week (May 8–14)
Fourth week (May 22–28)
During these periods, parts of northwest, central, and west India, along with the east coast, could experience intensified heatwave conditions.
However, relief is expected during:
First week (May 1–7)
Third week (May 15–21)
when rainfall, thunderstorms, and cloud cover are likely to keep temperatures relatively moderate.
Urban Discomfort and Coastal Humidity
The Minister also flagged higher nighttime temperatures as a concern, particularly in urban and coastal areas, which could increase discomfort levels. Additionally, hot and humid conditions are expected along the east coast, Gujarat and Maharashtra.
Government Preparedness and Advisory
Reassuring the public, Singh said authorities at state and district levels have been directed to ensure:
Adequate drinking water supply
Functional cooling arrangements
Uninterrupted power supply
Special focus is being placed on vulnerable groups such as infants, the elderly, and outdoor workers.

Farmers Advised to Plan Smartly
On the agriculture front, the Minister noted that conditions remain largely favourable for harvesting late Rabi crops in northern and northwestern regions. However, localized heat stress could impact summer crops like rice (Boro), maize, and pulses in parts of southern and northeastern India.
Farmers have been advised to:
Conduct fieldwork during morning and evening hours
Ensure light and frequent irrigation
Take protective measures for livestock
Follow Forecasts, Stay Safe
Issuing a public advisory, Singh urged citizens to stay hydrated, avoid direct sun exposure during peak hours, and closely follow updates from IMD through its official platforms and mobile applications.
